mnemonics.title: Del = Directional Derivative Driver
mnemonics.content: The del operator (∇) is like a driver that takes partial derivatives in all x, y, z directions simultaneously: ∇ = (∂/∂x, ∂/∂y, ∂/∂z)

mnemonics.title: Partial Pan — Take a Slice
mnemonics.content: The partial derivative symbol (∂) looks like a curved 'd'. Think of it as 'd' for derivative but we only take a 'pan' (slice) of one variable at a time while holding others constant

mnemonics.title: GRAD = Great Rate of Ascending Direction
mnemonics.content: Gradient (∇f) points in the direction of steepest ascent. Remember: GRAD = Greatest Rate of Ascending Derivative

mnemonics.title: DIV = Dot Product, Outward Flow
mnemonics.content: Divergence (∇·F) uses the DOT (·) product. Think of divergence as how much a vector field 'diverges' or spreads OUT from a point

mnemonics.title: CURL = Curl Uses Rotational Cross
mnemonics.content: Curl (∇×F) uses the CROSS (×) product. Think of curl as rotation — like water curling/spinning in a vortex

mnemonics.title: Chain Rule: Dances in a Circle
mnemonics.content: For chain rule: ∂z/∂x = (∂z/∂u)(∂u/∂x) + (∂z/∂v)(∂v/∂x). Remember: multiply along paths and ADD when paths merge (like rivers joining)

mnemonics.title: Nabla Backwards
mnemonics.content: ∇ (nabla) is delta (Δ) backwards. Delta means 'change' — nabla performs changes (derivatives) on functions

acronyms.usage: Used when taking derivative of a function with multiple variables, holding others constant. Example: ∂f/∂x means derivative of f with respect to x only
Vector differential operator: ∇ = (∂/∂x, ∂/∂y, ∂/∂z). Applied to scalar → gradient; applied to vector → divergence (dot) or curl (cross)
Equation involving partial derivatives. Used in graphics (Laplacian), physics simulations, and machine learning (gradient descent)
Notation for partial derivative of f with respect to x. fₓ = ∂f/∂x
Partial derivative taken twice with respect to x. Used for testing concavity and in Hessian matrices
∇² = ∂²/∂x² + ∂²/∂y² + ∂²/∂z². Used in image processing (edge detection), heat equations, and computer graphics

visualTips.visualization: Picture ∇ as a machine with three buttons: ∂/∂x, ∂/∂y, ∂/∂z. Feed it a function, and it spits out a vector of three partial derivatives
Imagine standing on a hill (scalar field). The gradient (∇f) is the arrow pointing straight uphill — the steepest path. Its magnitude tells you how steep
Picture a fountain (vector field). Divergence measures if water is spurting OUT (positive) or sucking IN (negative) at each point. Positive divergence = source, negative = sink
Watch water going down a drain — it curls/spins. Curl measures the rotation amount and axis of rotation in a vector field. Zero curl = irrotational (no spin)
Think of a 3D surface as a mountain. ∂f/∂x is the shadow you'd see looking from the side — the slope in only the x-direction, ignoring y changes
fₓᵧ = fᵧₓ (if continuous). Picture shuffling a deck of cards — no matter the order (XY or YX), you end up with the same sorted deck

analogies.analogy: Like adjusting ONE knob on a complex sound mixer while keeping all other knobs fixed. You're only measuring how changing one setting affects the final sound
Think of ∇ as a multi-tool Swiss Army knife for derivatives. It's one symbol but contains three operations (∂/∂x, ∂/∂y, ∂/∂z) ready to apply in different combinations
Like a compass needle always pointing uphill on a hiking trail. It tells you which direction to go to climb fastest and how steep that path is
Like measuring airflow at a vent. If air flows OUT in all directions, positive divergence. If air gets sucked IN, negative divergence
Like a weather vane spinning in a wind tunnel. Curl measures how fast and in what direction the wind makes things spin
Like converting currency through an intermediate currency. USD → EUR → INR. The final rate = (USD→EUR rate) × (EUR→ INR rate). Same with partials: ∂z/∂x = (∂z/∂u)(∂u/∂x)
Like measuring not just speed (first derivative) but acceleration (second derivative). fₓₓ tells you how the slope itself is changing in the x-direction
